item.title: Backend Transitioning to AI Dev
item.author: Sugam Agrawal
item.content:encoded: After working with LLMs, I believe the hardest part of the transition for backend engineers isn't the math—it's unlearning determinism.In traditional distributed systems, Input A always yields Output B. If it doesn't, it’s a bug. With GenAI, Input A might yield Output B today, and a completely different structure tomorrow.This breaks everything we know about stability at scale. You can't write a standard unit test for a "vibe check." You can't rely on a model to output valid JSON 100% of the time, even with strict prompting. You can't predict latency when the inference provider is overloaded.The solution isn't better prompt engineering; it's defensive architecture. We need to shift focus from "making the model perfect" to building resilient wrappers—schema validators, circuit breakers, and automated evaluation pipelines that catch regressions before users do.Treat the LLM like an untrusted, high-latency 3rd-party API, not a magic box.

item.title: Building an Autonomous Skill-Architecture Agent for Rapid Career Transition
item.author: Aniket Hingane
item.content:encoded:
How I Orchestrated Multi-Agent Systems to Automate Market Analysis and Personalized Curriculum DesignI observed a recurring frustration in professional circles: the sheer complexity of career pivoting in 2026. In my opinion, traditional learning platforms fail because they lack architectural context. To solve this, I built an experimental multi-agent PoC—the Autonomous Skill-Architecture Agent. As per my experience, using specialized agents for market pulse, gap analysis, and resource curation transforms a weeks-long manual research process into a 10-second automated execution.I wrote this article because I believe we are entering an era where learning isn't just about content; it's about architecture. I thought about how I used to manually scour LinkedIn and Coursera to understand what I needed to learn next. I put it this way because manual research is systematically biased. In my opinion, we need a self-correcting, autonomous system that understands market demand as well as we understand our own resumes.From my experience, the gap between "what I know" and "what the market wants" is often a black box. In my experiments, I've seen that breaking down this problem into distinct agentic roles—Market Analyst, Skill Gap Analyzer, Curriculum Architect—provides a level of precision that a human researcher simply cannot match. This isn't just another upskilling platform; it's my experiment in building a technical "bridge" for career transitions.
What's This Article About? I think it's important to clarify: this is an experimental PoC. I am exploring the intersection of multi-agent orchestration and EdTech. I wanted to see if I could build a system that takes a "Current Role" and a "Target Role" and outputs a high-fidelity, resource-validated learning path. In my opinion, the most practical use case for AI agents today is handling high-entropy research tasks. Career planning is exactly that. As per me, this project demonstrates how to structure such a system using simple Python logic and specialized roles.I chose this stack because I wanted maximum transparency and modularity. I didn't want a "black box" solution. : The backbone of my experiments. I used it for the agent logic and orchestration.: For the data visualization layer. I think visualizing gaps is as important as identifying them.: For the technical architecture and flow diagrams. From my experience, clear diagrams are the language of professional engineering.: To generate the hyper-realistic terminal animation. I put it this way because a static image doesn't capture the "live" feel of an autonomous agent.I think you should read this if you're interested in multi-agent systems but want to move beyond hello-world examples. I wrote this because I want to share my opinion on how to structure "Mission-Driven" agents. In my experience, most agent tutorials focus on chat. I'm focusing on . As per me, the value here is in the decomposition of a business problem. I've broken down "upskilling" into a pipeline that you can replicate for any domain—be it healthcare, finance, or retail.I put a lot of thought into the sequence of events. I didn't want the agents to just "talk." I wanted them to perform. In my opinion, the sequence of analysis is critical.I structured the flow like this because the Market Analyst must define the goalposts before the Skill Analyzer can even step on the field. From my experience, if you don't have a clear "Role Target," the gap analysis becomes generic and useless.I started by defining the base class. I thought it was important to include a method to simulate the communication overhead.In my opinion, every agent needs a role and a clear goal. I wrote the code this way to ensure that each agent stay within its lane.I added the because I wanted to observe the logs as they happened. I think it makes the "autonomy" feel more visceral.I then built the . From my experience, this is the hardest role to mock. I used a dictionary of trending roles, but in my opinion, this should eventually be a RAG-enabled agent scanning real-time job boards.I think the key here is the "Top 5" constraint. I observed that if you provide too many skills, the curriculum becomes overwhelming.
Visualization: The Skill Radar I put a lot of emphasis on the "Skill Radar" chart. In my opinion, a spider/radar chart is the best way to visualize a multi-dimensional gap. I used to create this.I chose the blue-ish color palette because it looks premium and professional. I think the visual proof of a "gap" makes the subsequent curriculum much more convincing to a user.I wanted to make this experiment as reproducible as possible. I wrote a step-by-step setup guide because I think nothing is worse than "broken PoCs."Clone the experiments repository.Initialize your local environment.Install the specific dependencies I've documented.Run the main orchestration.I observed the following output during my final execution. I think it's worth noting how the agents hand off information.🚀 INITIALIZING AUTONOMOUS SKILL-ARCHITECTURE AGENT Targeting Transition to: AI Engineer Current Skill Set: Python, SQL, Git, FastAPI [Market Pulse]: Top 5 required skills for 'AI Engineer' identified. [Gap Finder]: Found 3 critical skill gaps. [Roadmap Maker]: Learning path structured into 3 technical phases. [Source Finder]: Strategic resources mapped to all curriculum phases. In my opinion, the most impressive part is the ASCII table at the end. It's clean, technical, and provides an immediate roadmap.I think we are just scratching the surface of what autonomous architecture agents can do. I wrote this PoC to prove that career planning doesn't have to be a shot in the dark. In my opinion, when we leverage multi-agent systems, we aren't just automating tasks; we are automating .From my experience, the next step would be to integrate real-world course APIs and live job data. But for now, I'm happy with this experiment. I think it proves the viability of using agentic architecture for personal professional growth.The views and opinions expressed here are solely my own and do not represent the views, positions, or opinions of my employer or any organization I am affiliated with. The content is based on my personal experience and experimentation and may be incomplete or incorrect. Any errors or misinterpretations are unintentional, and I apologize in advance if any statements are misunderstood or misrepresented.MOST IMPORTANT: This project is an experimental PoC. I am highlighting these as personal experiments.

I've broken down "upskilling" into a pipeline that you can replicate for any domain—be it healthcare, finance, or retail.I put a lot of thought into the sequence of events. I didn't want the agents to just "talk." I wanted them to perform. In my opinion, the sequence of analysis is critical.I structured the flow like this because the Market Analyst must define the goalposts before the Skill Analyzer can even step on the field. From my experience, if you don't have a clear "Role Target," the gap analysis becomes generic and useless.I started by defining the base class. I thought it was important to include a method to simulate the communication overhead.In my opinion, every agent needs a role and a clear goal. I wrote the code this way to ensure that each agent stay within its lane.I added the because I wanted to observe the logs as they happened. I think it makes the "autonomy" feel more visceral.I then built the . From my experience, this is the hardest role to mock. I used a dictionary of trending roles, but in my opinion, this should eventually be a RAG-enabled agent scanning real-time job boards.I think the key here is the "Top 5" constraint. I observed that if you provide too many skills, the curriculum becomes overwhelming.
Visualization: The Skill Radar I put a lot of emphasis on the "Skill Radar" chart. In my opinion, a spider/radar chart is the best way to visualize a multi-dimensional gap. I used to create this.I chose the blue-ish color palette because it looks premium and professional. I think the visual proof of a "gap" makes the subsequent curriculum much more convincing to a user.I wanted to make this experiment as reproducible as possible. I wrote a step-by-step setup guide because I think nothing is worse than "broken PoCs."Clone the experiments repository.Initialize your local environment.Install the specific dependencies I've documented.Run the main orchestration.I observed the following output during my final execution. I think it's worth noting how the agents hand off information.🚀 INITIALIZING AUTONOMOUS SKILL-ARCHITECTURE AGENT Targeting Transition to: AI Engineer Current Skill Set: Python, SQL, Git, FastAPI [Market Pulse]: Top 5 required skills for 'AI Engineer' identified. [Gap Finder]: Found 3 critical skill gaps. [Roadmap Maker]: Learning path structured into 3 technical phases. [Source Finder]: Strategic resources mapped to all curriculum phases. In my opinion, the most impressive part is the ASCII table at the end. It's clean, technical, and provides an immediate roadmap.I think we are just scratching the surface of what autonomous architecture agents can do. I wrote this PoC to prove that career planning doesn't have to be a shot in the dark. In my opinion, when we leverage multi-agent systems, we aren't just automating tasks; we are automating .From my experience, the next step would be to integrate real-world course APIs and live job data. But for now, I'm happy with this experiment. I think it proves the viability of using agentic architecture for personal professional growth.The views and opinions expressed here are solely my own and do not represent the views, positions, or opinions of my employer or any organization I am affiliated with. The content is based on my personal experience and experimentation and may be incomplete or incorrect. Any errors or misinterpretations are unintentional, and I apologize in advance if any statements are misunderstood or misrepresented.MOST IMPORTANT: This project is an experimental PoC. I am highlighting these as personal experiments.

item.title: The Secret Life of Python: The Loophole
item.author: Aaron Rose
item.content:encoded:
The trap of modifying a list while iterating over it (and how to fix it).The library was warm, but Timothy was sweating. He was staring at a list of sensor data on his screen."I don't understand," he muttered. "I told it to remove the errors. I wrote the code explicitly. But it keeps missing them."Margaret glided over, placing a fresh pot of tea on the table. "Missing them?""Yes," Timothy said. "I have a list of temperature readings. Any reading below zero is an error—a glitch. I wrote a loop to find them and delete them.""Watch," Timothy said. "I have two negative numbers: and . The loop should catch both."Removing glitch: -5 Cleaned List: [10, -2, 15, 20]
Timothy threw his hands up. "It missed the ! It looked right at it and walked on by. Is the loop broken?""The loop is not broken," Margaret said calmly. "The floor is moving."Margaret pulled a few books off the shelf and laid them in a row on the table.[Book A] [Book B] [Book C] [Book D]"Imagine these are your list items," she said. "The loop uses an internal pointer—an index—to track where it is. It starts at Index 0."She pointed to the first book. "Step 0." Then she moved her finger. "Step 1: The negative number.""Now," Margaret said, "you tell Python to the item at Step 1." She pulled out of the line.Immediately, she slid the remaining books to the left to fill the gap.[Book A] [Book C] [Book D]"What just happened?" she asked."Everything shifted left," Timothy said."Exactly. The item that at Index 2 () has now slid into Index 1. But the loop doesn't know the floor moved. The loop just finishes Step 1 and says, 'Okay, time for Step 2'."She moved her finger to the Index 2 ()."See?" Margaret whispered. "You skipped . Because you destroyed the step you were standing on, the next step fell into the hole, and you stepped right over it.""That's why it missed the !" Timothy realized. "It slid into the slot I just cleared, and the loop jumped past it.""So I can never modify a list loop?" Timothy asked."You can," Margaret said. "But you must not modify the list you are walking on. You must walk on a map, and modify the territory."She deleted his loop and typed a subtle change.Cleaned List: [10, 15, 20]
"By adding ," Margaret explained, "you iterate over a of the list—the same trick we used to avoid aliasing, but here it serves a different purpose. The loop walks safely on the copy—which never changes—while you delete items from the original list. The indices on your copy never shift, so you never skip a beat.""Is there a better way?" Timothy asked. "Removing items one by one feels... slow.""There is," Margaret smiled. "Instead of fixing a broken world, simply build a new one."She showed him the approach."This is often faster and cleaner," she said. "We don't modify. We just filter."Margaret opened her notebook to the "Loops" section, drawing a warning sign. Never add or remove items from a list you are looping over it. Skipped items (if removing) or infinite loops (if adding). When you change the list length, the indices shift, but the loop counter keeps marching forward. (Quick fix).[x for x in my_list if condition] (Best for filtering).for item in reversed(my_list): (Advanced—useful when you modify in-place without copying).Timothy looked at his clean data. "I feel like I was trying to change a tire while the car was moving.""Worse," Margaret replied, pouring the tea. "You were trying to remove the floorboards while dancing on them."In the next episode, Margaret and Timothy will face "The Lazy Baker"—where they discover that some "lists" aren't lists at all, but lazy machines (generators) waiting to produce data on demand.

item.title: Building Blackjack for the Terminal: My First Python Project
item.author: Jonathan Key
item.content:encoded:
For my latest programming assignment from Codecademy, I decided to build a game. I chose this project because I wanted to practice translating real-world rules (like the complex logic of an Ace being either 1 or 11) into clean, functional Python code.My goal was to create a game that felt interactive and "smart" enough to play against a dealer, all within a simple text-based interface.Here is a look at the gameplay loop, showing the deck shuffling, the deal, and the logic behind hitting and standing:This project was a great way to dive deeper into Python's core features. Here are a few technical highlights from the build: I used a to map card ranks (like 'J', 'Q', 'K') to their numeric values. This made it easy to efficiently calculate hand totals. To ensure every game is different, I used Python’s module. This replicates a real deck being mixed before it is dealt. The core of the game is a loop that keeps the "Hit or Stand" phase active until the player either stops or "busts" (goes over 21). One of the trickiest parts was the Ace logic. I implemented logic to check whether a hand exceeds 21 and automatically adjust an Ace's value from 11 to 1 if necessary.
Check Out the Source Code You can explore the full logic, including how I handled the dealer's AI and the game flow, on my GitHub:Building this game taught me that the hardest part of coding isn't just writing syntax, it's planning for every possible user decision. Seeing the logic come together to successfully crown a winner (or bust a player!) was incredibly satisfying.I'm looking forward to adding more features, like a or a to support multiple decks, in the future!

item.title: End-to-End MLOps Project: Customer Churn Prediction
item.author: Koteswararao J
item.content:encoded:
Build a complete machine learning pipeline to predict customer churn with automated training, versioning, testing, and deployment.: Scikit-learn, XGBoost: MLflow: Git, DVC (Data Version Control): FastAPI + Docker: Prometheus + Grafana (optional): AWS/GCP/Azure (we'll use AWS)customer-churn-mlops/ ├── data/ │ ├── raw/ │ └── processed/ ├── notebooks/ │ └── exploratory_analysis.ipynb ├── src/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── data_preprocessing.py │ ├── feature_engineering.py │ ├── model_training.py │ └── model_evaluation.py ├── api/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── main.py │ └── schemas.py ├── tests/ │ ├── test_preprocessing.py │ └── test_model.py ├── models/ ├── .github/ │ └── workflows/ │ ├── ci.yml │ └── cd.yml ├── Dockerfile ├── requirements.txt ├── config.yaml ├── dvc.yaml └── README.md
Step 1: Setup & Data Preparation customer-churn-mlops customer-churn-mlops
git init
python venv venv venv/bin/activate pip pandas numpy scikit-learn xgboost mlflow dvc fastapi uvicorn pytest pip freeze requirements.txt
Step 2: Feature Engineering
Step 3: Model Training with MLflow
Step 4: Configuration File
Step 5: DVC Setup (Data Version Control)
dvc init
dvc add data/raw/churn.csv
Step 6: FastAPI Deployment pip requirements.txt
Step 8: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
Step 10: Running the Complete Pipeline
python src/model_training.py
mlflow ui 5000
uvicorn api.main:app curl POST docker build churn-prediction:latest docker run 8000:8000 churn-prediction:latest
curl http://localhost:8000/health
Production Deployment (AWS)
aws ecr create-repository churn-prediction
docker build churn-prediction:latest docker tag churn-prediction:latest <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/churn-prediction:latest docker push <account-id>.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/churn-prediction:latest
Step 11: Monitoring & Logging
Project Completion Checklist [ ] Data preprocessing pipeline[ ] Model training with MLflow tracking[ ] Model versioning with DVC[ ] Docker containerization: Git for code, DVC for data and models: MLflow for model parameters and metrics: FastAPI for serving predictions: Docker for reproducible environments: GitHub Actions for CI/CD: Pytest for quality assurance: Logging and metrics for production
Next Steps & Improvements Add A/B testing frameworkImplement model retraining pipelineSet up Grafana dashboardsAdd model explainability (SHAP/LIME)This project demonstrates a production-ready MLOps pipeline suitable for real-world applications!

item.title: Sensor Fusion Explained: How to Build a High-Accuracy Fitness Rep Counter
item.author: wellallyTech
item.content:encoded:
Fitness apps are everywhere, yet many fail at one fundamental task: accurately counting repetitions for complex movements. If you have ever seen an app count "phantom" reps or miss a set entirely, you have seen the limits of single-sensor tracking.The accelerometers in our phones are naturally , while gyroscopes suffer from over time. To solve this, developers use "sensor fusion" to create a stable, reliable signal. For a look at the visuals and code structure behind this tech, see this comprehensive rep counter guide.To build a high-performance tracker, we must understand why using just one sensor usually fails during a workout.: This sensor measures proper acceleration, including gravity. While it is stable over long periods, it is incredibly sensitive to vibrations and sudden shakes.: This measures how fast a device rotates. It is excellent for tracking quick changes but suffers from "drift," where small errors accumulate until the orientation is completely wrong.By fusing these two together, we get the short-term precision of the gyroscope and the long-term stability of the accelerometer.
The Logic of the Complementary Filter A is a lightweight way to merge these signals without the high processing power required by complex math like Kalman filters. The formula is straightforward: FusedAngle = α * (Gyro Angle) + (1 - α) * (Accel Angle). Usually, we set alpha to , meaning we trust the gyroscope for 98% of the data but use the accelerometer to "pull" the signal back to reality.
Building a Smart State Machine Once we have a clean signal, we don't just count every "peak." Instead, we use a to track the phases of an exercise like a bicep curl.: The user is at rest.: The angle crosses a specific entry threshold (e.g., 30 degrees).: The user reaches the peak and begins the return movement.: Once the return is complete, the count increments and resets to IDLE.This multi-step logic ensures that small jitters or mid-rep pauses don't lead to false counts, making the app feel much more professional and "intelligent."
Accuracy in Every Movement This framework is highly adaptable to various exercises beyond the bicep curl. For , you can track the torso's pitch angle, while rely on the vertical movement and thigh orientation.The demand for real-time, accurate health feedback is growing rapidly. Mastering sensor fusion allows you to build tools that users can actually trust for their daily progress. To see the Python implementation and start your own project, read WellAlly’s full guide.

item.title: 🛡️ Build a Smart Excel Data Cleaner in Python (Step-by-Step)
item.author: Mate Technologies
item.content:encoded:
A beginner-friendly walkthrough of SmartExcelGuardian – a professional Excel data cleaning and reporting tool built with Python.A desktop GUI app for Excel cleanupAutomatic missing value handlingHeuristic “data health” scoringExport results to Excel, PDF, JSON, and TXTpip install pandas numpy openpyxl ttkbootstrap reportlab📁 Project Structure SmartExcelGuardian/ │── logo.ico1️⃣ Import Required LibrariesWe start by importing everything we need.import os, sys, threading, json import tkinter as tk from tkinter import filedialog import ttkbootstrap as tb from ttkbootstrap.constants import * from datetime import datetime ttkbootstrap → modern dark UIthreading → keep UI responsive during cleanupimport pandas as pd import numpy as np import re from openpyxl import Workbook from openpyxl.styles import PatternFill, Font from openpyxl.utils.dataframe import dataframe_to_rows Excel export & formatting (openpyxl)Column name normalization (re)from reportlab.pdfgen import canvas from reportlab.lib.pagesizes import A4 from reportlab.lib.units import mm from reportlab.lib.colors import red, orange, green, black Used for generating professional PDF reports.2️⃣ Global State & Loggingstop_event = threading.Event() cleanup_results = {} log_file = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "excelguardian.log") stop_event → allows canceling cleanupcleanup_results → shared export datalog_file → error tracking3️⃣ Utility Helper Functions Resource Loader (for packaged apps)def resource_path(file_name): base_path = getattr(sys, "_MEIPASS", os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))) return os.path.join(base_path, file_name) This helps when bundling with PyInstaller.def clean_column_name(name): name = name.strip().lower() name = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", "", name) name = re.sub(r"\s+", "_", name) return name Original Cleaned Total Sales ($) total_salesdef convert_numpy(obj): if isinstance(obj, np.integer): return int(obj) if isinstance(obj, np.floating): return float(obj) if isinstance(obj, np.ndarray): return obj.tolist() raise TypeError Needed because JSON can’t serialize NumPy types.4️⃣ Creating the Main Windowapp = tb.Window(themename="darkly") app.title("SmartExcelGuardian v1.1.0") app.geometry("1100x650") tb.Label(app, text="SmartExcelGuardian", font=("Segoe UI", 22, "bold")).pack(pady=(10, 2))
tb.Label(app, text="Professional Excel Data Guardian Tool", font=("Segoe UI", 10, "italic"), foreground="#9ca3af").pack(pady=(0, 8)) This creates the app header.file_path = tk.StringVar()
tb.Entry(row1, textvariable=file_path, width=60).pack(side="left", padx=6)
tb.Button( row1, text="📄 Excel File", command=lambda: file_path.set( filedialog.askopenfilename(filetypes=[("Excel Files", "*.xlsx *.xls")]) ) ).pack(side="left") This lets users choose their Excel workbook.7️⃣ Cleanup Control Buttonsstart_btn = tb.Button(row2, text="🛡 CLEAN DATA", bootstyle="success") stop_btn = tb.Button(row2, text="🛑 STOP", bootstyle="danger-outline", state="disabled") CLEAN DATA → starts background threadSTOP → safely halts processing8️⃣ Results Table (TreeView)cols = ( "column", "original_type", "suggested_type", "cleaned_type", "missing_values", "duplicates_detected", "heuristic_score", "rename_suggestion" )
tree = tb.Treeview(row3, columns=cols, show="headings") This displays column-by-column health analysis.9️⃣ Heuristic Scoring Systemdef heuristic_score(missing, duplicates, type_issue): score = 0 score += min(30, missing * 2) score += min(30, duplicates * 2) score += 40 if type_issue else 0 return min(score, 100) Score Meaning Score Risk 31–70 Moderate 🟠 🔟 Data Cleaning Enginedef assess_and_clean(df): for col in df.columns: series = df[col]
coerced = pd.to_numeric(series, errors="coerce") cleaned_series = coerced.fillna(coerced.mean()) ✔ Converts text → numbers ✔ Fills missing values with meancleaned_series = series.astype("string").fillna(series.mode()[0]) ✔ Converts to strings ✔ Fills missing values with most common value1️⃣1️⃣ Threaded Cleanup Executionthreading.Thread( target=run_cleanup, daemon=True ).start()
Prevents freezing on large Excel files1️⃣2️⃣ Excel Export with Formulassum_formula = f"=SUM(A2:A{ws.max_row})" mean_formula = f"=AVERAGE(A2:A{ws.max_row})" 1️⃣3️⃣ Conditional Formattingfill = PatternFill(start_color="FF9999", fill_type="solid") cell.font = Font(bold=True) def score_color(score): if score >= 71: return red elif score >= 31: return orange else: return green Creates a multi-page PDF audit report with:1️⃣5️⃣ About & Help Windowtb.Label(frame, text="How to Use", font=("Segoe UI", 12, "bold")) A professional Excel cleanerA heuristic scoring systemAdd charts (data health trends)

item.title: # Building a Clean REST API in Go: From Spaghetti Code to Clean Architecture
item.author: Paul Babatuyi
item.content:encoded:
I'll be honest—my first REST API in Go was a disaster. Everything lived in : database queries, business logic, HTTP handlers, all tangled together in a 1,500-line monstrosity. Adding a feature meant scrolling through endless code, hoping I wouldn't break something unrelated.Then I discovered , and it changed everything. This is the story of how I rebuilt my inventory management API with proper separation of concerns, making it actually maintainable. No fluff, no theory-only content—just the real lessons from building .
The Problem: Why I Needed Better Architecture Picture this: you need to add OAuth login to your API. In my original spaghetti code, I'd have to:Hunt through for where users are createdHope the password hashing wasn't hardcoded somewhere random Add OAuth logic... where? Next to the database code? In a new file? Who knows!Cross your fingers that you didn't break existing email/password loginSound familiar? That pain drove me to redesign everything with .
The Solution: Clean Architecture in Go Here's the key insight that clicked for me: separate what something does from how it does it.Now, when PM asks for OAuth? I just add a new method in . The handler and repository don't even know it happened. Beautiful.
The Architecture: A Tour Through the Layers Let me walk you through how requests flow through BMG. I'll use a real example: creating an inventory item.
Layer 1: Handler (The HTTP Bouncer) Convert HTTP requests into something the business logic can understand.❌ No business rules ("quantity can't be negative")❌ No password hashing or complex logicJust: receive HTTP → call service → return HTTP. That's it.
Layer 2: Service (The Brain) Enforce business rules, orchestrate complex operations.This layer knows should happen, but not it happens. It doesn't care if we're using PostgreSQL, MongoDB, or a text file—that's the repository's problem.
Layer 3: Repository (The Database Whisperer) Talk to the database. Nothing else.All the SQL lives here. If we switch from PostgreSQL to MySQL tomorrow, we only change this file. The service and handler don't even know we use a database.
The Secret Sauce: DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) Here's something that confused me for months: why not just use domain models everywhere? Don't accidentally leak sensitive fields API shape doesn't force database schema Support multiple API versions easilyI learned this the hard way when I accidentally returned password hashes in endpoint. Good times.
Middleware: The Pipeline Pattern Middleware in Go is elegant. Each request passes through a chain of functions before hitting your handler:Request → Logger → CORS → RateLimit → Auth → Handler → Response Here's how simple it is with Chi router:
The Middleware I Wish I'd Built Earlier Seeing "POST /items 245ms" in logs saved my bacon when debugging slow requests.Prevented a junior dev's infinite loop from killing the server. True story.
Error Handling: The Go Way Go's error handling gets mocked, but I've grown to love it. Here's my approach:The key: errors flow up, decisions flow down. Services return errors, handlers decide HTTP status codes.
Database: Connection Pooling That Actually Works This took me embarrassingly long to get right:Went from 200ms queries to 15ms just by fixing this. Connection overhead is real.
Testing: What Actually Gets Tested I don't test everything. Hot take, I know. Here's what I test:1. Business Logic (Service Layer)2. Repository Integration Tests Handlers. They're just glue code. If the service works and the repository works, the handler will work.
Performance: The Numbers That Matter Here's what I learned from production: 45ms (10x improvement!)What made the difference: (biggest win) (prevents slow queries from piling up) (added indexes on frequently queried columns) (auth before expensive operations)
Deployment: From Local to Production The beauty of this architecture? It deploys anywhere. go run cmd/api/main.go go build api cmd/api/main.go
Same binary, different config. That's the power of the 12-factor app.
Lessons Learned (The Hard Way) 1. Start with layers from day one Refactoring spaghetti code is 10x harder than starting clean.2. DTOs are worth the boilerplate Yes, it's extra typing. No, it's not premature optimization. Saved me from leaking sensitive data.3. Middleware ordering matters4. Context is your friendPrevents runaway queries from killing your server. My first attempt had 5 layers. Ridiculous. Three is plenty: handler, service, repository.This is just v1. The architecture makes it easy to add:[ ] OAuth2 integration (just add a new auth method in service)[ ] Caching with Redis (add a cache layer, repository doesn't change)[ ] Event-driven features (publish events from service layer)[ ] gRPC endpoints (reuse the service layer!)That last one is key: good architecture is protocol-agnostic. Want both REST and gRPC? Just add handlers. Your business logic stays the same.Clone it. Break it. Make it better. That's how we all learn.cmd/api/ # HTTP server entrypoint internal/ ├── handler/ # HTTP → Service ├── service/ # Business logic ├── repository/ # Service → Database ├── domain/ # Core entities └── dto/ # API contracts make migrate-up make run curl localhost:4000/v1/healthcheck Clean architecture isn't about following rules religiously. It's about making your future self's life easier.When you get that 3 AM support call because something broke, you want to know exactly where to look. Handler? Service? Repository? Clear boundaries = faster debugging.When PM wants "just a small feature" that turns into refactoring half the codebase, you want layers that prevent cascading changes.When you're onboarding a new dev, you want a structure so obvious they can contribute on day one.That's what this architecture gave me. Hope it helps you too.Questions? Disagree with my approach? Drop a comment. I'm especially curious about how others handle testing—I know my approach is minimal, and I'd love to hear alternatives. Star the repo and follow me for more backend deep dives. Next up: adding OAuth2 to this exact API. Check out my UploadStream project where I use the same layered approach for high-performance file streaming.

item.title: **Navigating the Complexities of Managing a Minor's Trust: A Guide for Guardians and Relatives**
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Navigating the Complexities of Managing a Minor's Trust: A Guide for Guardians and RelativesAs a guardian or relative entrusted with managing a minor's trust, you may face a multitude of challenges, including navigating the complexities of family dynamics and making informed investment decisions. In this article, we will delve into the intricacies of managing a minor's trust, providing guidance on how to balance the needs of the beneficiary with the concerns of interfering relatives.Understanding the Role of a Trust GuardianAs the guardian of an $80,000 trust for a 15-year-old relative, you have a significant responsibility to manage the funds in a way that benefits the beneficiary's long-term financial well-being. This includes making investment decisions, paying taxes, and ensuring that the trust is administered in accordance with the terms of the trust agreement.Dealing with Interfering RelativesIt's not uncommon for relatives to express concerns or offer unsolicited advice when it comes to managing a minor's trust. While their intentions may be well-meaning, their interference can create tension and undermine your ability to make informed decisions. To address this issue, consider the following strategies:Establish clear communication channels: Set clear boundaries and expectations with relatives regarding your role as trust guardian and the decision-making process. This will help prevent misunderstandings and ensure that everyone is on the same page.: Keep relatives informed about the trust's performance, investment decisions, and any significant events that may impact the beneficiary's financial situation.: If relatives continue to express concerns or offer unsolicited advice, consider seeking guidance from a financial advisor or attorney who can provide expert advice and help mediate any disputes.Focus on the beneficiary's best interests: Remember that your primary responsibility is to act in the best interests of the beneficiary. Make decisions that align with their long-term financial goals and well-being, rather than trying to appease relatives.Teaching Good Money HabitsIn addition to managing the trust, you may also want to teach your young relative good money habits and financial literacy skills. This can be a valuable opportunity to instill a strong understanding of personal finance and help them develop healthy financial habits.Encourage financial education: Consider enrolling your relative in a financial education program or course that teaches basic money management skills, such as budgeting, saving, and investing.Model good financial behavior: Demonstrate responsible financial habits yourself, such as saving, investing, and avoiding debt.Involve the beneficiary in decision-making: As the beneficiary grows older, involve them in the decision-making process, allowing them to take ownership of their financial decisions and develop a sense of responsibility.Managing a minor's trust can be a complex and challenging task, particularly when dealing with interfering relatives. By establishing clear communication channels, providing regular updates, seeking professional advice, and focusing on the beneficiary's best interests, you can navigate these challenges and make informed investment decisions that benefit the beneficiary's long-term financial well-being. Additionally, by teaching good money habits and financial literacy skills, you can help your young relative develop a strong understanding of personal finance and set them up for long-term financial success.

item.title: **Title:** BlackRock's Stellar Performance in 2023: What Can Investors Expect in 2026?
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: BlackRock's Stellar Performance in 2023: What Can Investors Expect in 2026? BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, has consistently demonstrated its resilience and adaptability in the ever-changing landscape of the global financial markets. The company's recent earnings report has provided valuable insights into its performance in 2023, and we will delve into the key highlights and implications for investors. As we look ahead to 2026, it is essential to understand the factors that contributed to BlackRock's success and how they may shape the company's future prospects.A Year of Exceptional GrowthBlackRock's 2023 earnings report has been hailed as one of the strongest years in the company's history. The asset manager's net income rose by 15% year-over-year, driven by a combination of factors, including:Increased Assets Under Management (AUM): BlackRock's AUM grew by 12% in 2023, reaching a record high of $9.5 trillion. This significant increase in assets has provided the company with a substantial source of revenue.Strong Investment Performance: BlackRock's investment products have delivered impressive returns, with many funds outperforming their benchmarks. This has helped to attract new investors and retain existing ones.Diversification and Expansion: The company has continued to expand its product offerings and geographic presence, solidifying its position as a global leader in the asset management industry.Key Takeaways from the Earnings ReportThe earnings report has provided several key takeaways that are relevant to investors:: BlackRock's revenue grew by 14% year-over-year, driven by the increase in AUM and strong investment performance.: The company's operating expenses rose by 10% year-over-year, primarily due to increased investment in technology and talent.: BlackRock's ROE remained stable at 24.5%, indicating the company's ability to generate strong returns for its shareholders.What Can Investors Expect in 2026?As we look ahead to 2026, investors can expect several factors to shape BlackRock's performance:: The company's strong track record of growth is expected to continue, driven by the increasing demand for asset management services.: BlackRock is likely to continue investing in technology and expanding its product offerings to stay ahead of the competition.: The company will need to navigate the evolving regulatory landscape, which may impact its business operations and profitability.BlackRock's stellar performance in 2023 has provided valuable insights into the company's strengths and weaknesses. As we look ahead to 2026, investors can expect the company to continue growing and innovating, driven by its strong track record and commitment to excellence. By understanding the factors that contributed to BlackRock's success and how they may shape the company's future prospects, investors can make informed decisions about their investments in the asset management industry.

Among the pioneers in this space is Chai Discovery, a startup that has been making waves with its cutting-edge AI technology and strategic partnerships. In this article, we will delve into the journey of Chai Discovery, from its humble beginnings in OpenAI's offices to its recent deal with Eli Lilly, one of the world's leading pharmaceutical companies.Chai Discovery's story began in the offices of OpenAI, a renowned AI research organization. The startup's founders, who were part of OpenAI's team, recognized the vast potential of AI in transforming the pharmaceutical industry. They saw an opportunity to leverage AI's capabilities in drug discovery, a process that has traditionally relied on trial and error. With a shared vision, the team set out to create a platform that could accelerate the discovery of new medicines using AI.Chai Discovery's growth was fueled by strategic partnerships and investments from some of Silicon Valley's most influential venture capital firms. These partnerships not only provided the necessary funding but also brought valuable expertise and connections to the table. The startup's ability to attract top talent and secure significant investments has been a testament to its innovative approach and potential for impact.One of the most significant milestones in Chai Discovery's journey was its partnership with Eli Lilly, a global leader in pharmaceutical research and development. This collaboration marked a significant step forward in the adoption of AI-powered drug development. By combining Chai Discovery's AI technology with Eli Lilly's expertise in pharmaceutical research, the two companies aim to accelerate the discovery of new medicines and improve patient outcomes.Chai Discovery's rise to prominence in the AI-powered drug development space is a testament to the power of innovation and collaboration. From its humble beginnings in OpenAI's offices to its recent deal with Eli Lilly, the startup has demonstrated its potential to transform the pharmaceutical industry. As the field of AI-powered drug development continues to evolve, Chai Discovery is poised to play a leading role in shaping the future of medicine.Chai Discovery's AI technology has the potential to revolutionize the pharmaceutical industry.The startup's partnerships with influential VCs and Eli Lilly have provided significant funding and expertise.Chai Discovery's collaboration with Eli Lilly marks a significant step forward in the adoption of AI-powered drug development.As the healthcare industry continues to grapple with the challenges of developing new medicines, Chai Discovery's AI-powered platform is poised to make a significant impact. With its innovative approach and strategic partnerships, the startup is well-positioned to drive the next wave of innovation in pharmaceutical research and development.

item.title: How I Built a Daily Streak Keeper with Python & GitHub Actions
item.author: Rodney Gitonga
item.content:encoded: A guide on automating GitHub activity to keep your contribution streak alive using Python and GitHub Actions.🚀 How I Built a Daily Streak Keeper with Python & GitHub ActionsWe've all been there—staring at that one gray square in our GitHub contribution graph that broke a perfect streak. Whether you're busy, sick, or just forgot to push code, losing a streak can be demotivating.I decided to solve this problem by automating a daily commit using Python and GitHub Actions. Here is how I built my Daily Streak Keeper. Fetch a daily quote (to make the commit meaningful). Update the README.md with the new quote. Commit and push the changes automatically every day. Python: To fetch the quote and update the file. GitHub Actions: To schedule the script to run daily.The Python Script ()I wrote a simple Python script that fetches a random quote from an API and updates the file. I used for stability.The Automation ()Next, I needed this script to run automatically. GitHub Actions is perfect for this. I set up a workflow that runs every day at 12:00 PM Nairobi Time (09:00 UTC).This was a fun little project to ensure my profile stays active while also giving me a nice quote to read every day. It’s a great example of how powerful and easy GitHub Actions can be for automating simple tasks.Check out the code in the repository and feel free to fork it to create your own streak keeper! REPO

item.title: Why integer objects in python are not iterable
item.author: Onaolapo-11
item.content:encoded: Day 81 [January 16th, 2025]I need to buckle down, as I'm still lagging on day day 3 & 4 goals, "Day 3-4: Control structures (if-else, loops)", as well as day 5 (and 6) goals, "Day 5-6: Functions and modules", and Day 7 target (exercises) (Meta AI, personal communication, August 8, 2025). If I haven't covered this, I can't make progress on day 8 - 80 goals.Goals: As extracted from the 'Python for Software Development' textbook by Halvorsen (n.d.):Creating Functions in Python - IntroductionFunctions with multiple return valuesCreating Classes in PythonNotes: Python for Data Science, AI & Development Course (IBM) (Santarcangelo, n.d.): Module 3: Python Programming FundamentalsWhile doing the Hands-on-Lab for the topic on Loops (Santarcangelo, n.d.),After inputting this code: dates = [1997,2001,20003]for i in N: print(dates[1])TypeError Traceback (most recent call last) Cell In[8], line 4 1 dates = [1997,2001,20003] 2 N = len(dates) 5 print(dates[1])TypeError: 'int' object is not iterableSo a question came to mind: 'Why are integer objects in python not iterable?' I found this:Why integer objects in python are not iterable (Ramakrishna, 2021):Contain singular integer valuesDo not have '' methodprint(dir(int)) shows if an object contains '' method or not (Ramakrishna, 2021).Summary: I learnt why integer objects in python are not iterable.

item.title: How to Build a Monitoring Application Using Golang
item.author: wole
item.content:encoded:
Monitoring is a critical part of running reliable software, yet many teams only discover outages after users complaints starts rolling in. Imagine you get a Slack message at 2 AM, telling you that your APIs are down for over an hour and nobody noticed until customers started complaining. A monitoring service solves this problem by letting you and your team proactively respond to incidents, before problems escalate.In this tutorial, I will be taking you through the steps on how to build a status monitoring application from scratch. By the end of this article, you will have a system that:Probes your services on a schedule (HTTP, TCP, DNS, and more)Detects outages and sends alerts to various communication channels (Teams, Slack, etc)Tracks incidents with automatic open/closeExposes metrics for Prometheus and Grafana dashboardsFor this application, I will be using Go because it is fast, compiles to a single binary for cross platform support, and handles concurrency, which is important for an application that needs to monitor multiple endpoints simultaneously. We will be building a Go application "StatusD". It reads a config file that has a list of services to monitor, probes them, and creates incidents, fire notifications when something goes wrong.Grafana (Prometheus for metric)Here's the high-level architecture:┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Docker Compose │ │ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ Postgres │ │Prometheus│ │ Grafana │ │ Nginx │ │ │ │ DB │ │ (metrics)│ │(dashboard)│ │ (reverse proxy) │ │ │ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘ └────────┬─────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────┴─────────────┴──────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─────────┴─────────┐ │ │ │ StatusD │ │ │ │ (our Go app) │ │ │ └─────────┬─────────┘ │ │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ │Service │ │Service │ │Service │ │ A │ │ B │ │ C │ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ Before we write code, let's understand how the pieces fit together. Below is our project structure:status-monitor/ ├── cmd/statusd/ │ └── main.go # Application entry point ├── internal/ │ ├── models/ │ │ └── models.go # Data structures (Asset, Incident, etc.) │ ├── probe/ │ │ ├── probe.go # Probe registry │ │ └── http.go # HTTP probe implementation │ ├── scheduler/ │ │ └── scheduler.go # Worker pool and scheduling │ ├── alert/ │ │ └── engine.go # State machine and notifications │ ├── notifier/ │ │ └── teams.go # Teams/Slack integration │ ├── store/ │ │ └── postgres.go # Database layer │ ├── api/ │ │ └── handlers.go # REST API │ └── config/ │ └── manifest.go # Config loading ├── config/ │ ├── manifest.json # Services to monitor │ └── notifiers.json # Notification channels ├── migrations/ │ └── 001_init_schema.up.sql ├── docker-compose.yml ├── Dockerfile └── entrypoint.sh Here we will be defining our 'types', which essentially means we will be defining what a "monitored service" looks like.We will be defining four 'types':: This is a service we want to monitor.: What happens when we check an Asset; the response, latency, etc.: This tracks when something goes wrong, i.e., when ProbeResult returns an unexpected response (and when the service recovers).: This is an alert or message sent to the defined communications channel, e.g. Teams, Slack, email, etc.Lets define the types in code:Notice the field in the Asset type. Not all endpoints return 200, some may return 204 or a redirect. This lets you define what "healthy" means for each service.We need a place to store the probe results and incidents. We will be using PostgreSQL for this and here's our schema:The key insight is on probe_events(asset_id, timestamp DESC). Here, we are indexing by asset and timestamp (in a descending order), which allows us to quickly query for the probe results of a service.
Building the Probe System Things begin to get interesting here. We want to support probing over multiple protocol types: HTTPS, TCP, DNS, etc. without having to write a complex switch statement. To solve this, we are using a registry pattern.First we'll define what a probe looks like:Now implement the HTTP probe:The init() function runs automatically when your Go application starts. This adds the HTTP probe to the registry without any code change. Want to add TCP probes? Create , implement the interface, and register it in .
Scheduling and Concurrency We need to probe all our Assets on a schedule and for this we will be using a worker pool. A worker pool lets us run multiple probes concurrently without spawning a goroutine for each service.Each asset gets its own ticker goroutine that only schedules work. When its time to check an asset, the ticker sends a probe job into a channel. There are a fixed number of worker goroutines that listen on the channel and do the actual probing.We don't run probes directly in the ticker goroutines because probes can block while waiting for network responses or timeouts. By using workers, we can control concurrency. For example, with 4 workers and 100 assets, only 4 probes will run at any moment even if tickers fire simultaneously. The channel acts as a buffer for pending jobs, and a ensures all workers shut down cleanly.
Incident Detection: The State Machine When a probe fails, we don't automatically assume a failure. It could be network glitch. However, if it fails again, we create an incident. When it recovers, we close the incident and notify.This is a state machine: UP → DOWN → UP.Key insight: We track the state in memory for fast lookups, but persists incidents to the database for durability. If the process restarts, we can rebuild state from open incidents.In the event that something breaks, people need to know. We need to send the notification to various communication channels. Let's define our Teams notifier:Teams uses Adaptive Cards for rich formatting. You can define various notifiers for other communications channel, e.g. Slack, Discord, etc.We need endpoints to query the status of the services we are monitoring. For this, we will be using Chi, which is a lightweight router that supports route parameters like .The code above define a small HTTP API server, which exposes 4 read-only endpoint:GET /api/health - Health check (is the service running?) GET /api/assets - List all monitored services GET /api/assets/{id}/events - Get probe history for a specific service GET /api/incidents - List open incidents
Dockerizing the Application Dockerizing the application is pretty straighforward since Go compiles to a single binary. We are going to be using a multi-stage build to keep the final image small:apk add git go mod download 0 linux go build statusd ./cmd/statusd/
apk add ca-certificates +x /app/entrypoint.sh
The builder stage compiles the code. The final stage is just Alpine plus our binary—typically under 20MB.The entrypoint script builds the database connection string from environment variables: ./statusd /app/config/manifest.json /app/config/notifiers.json 4 8080
Docker Compose: Putting It All Together One file to rule them all:: The service waits until Postgres is actually ready, not just started. This prevents "connection refused" errors on first boot.: We mount as read-only. Edit your manifest locally, and the running container sees the changes.: Routes external traffic to Grafana and Prometheus dashboards.The application reads two files: and The file lists the assets we want to monitor. Each asset needs an ID, a probe type, and an address. The controls how often we check (60 = once per minute). lets you define what "healthy" means. Some endpoints return 301 redirects or 204 No Content, and that's fine. The controls where to send alerts. You define notification channels (Teams, Slack), then set policies for which channels fire on which events. means you won't get spammed more than once every 5 minutes for the same issue. That's it. Five services spin up: stores your data probes your services collects metricsLoading assets manifest... Loaded 2 assets Loading notifiers config... Loaded 1 notifiers Connecting to database... Starting scheduler... [✓] Production API (api-prod): 45ms [✓] Production Website (web-prod): 120ms You now have a monitoring system that:Reads services from a JSON configProbes them on a schedule using a worker poolDetects outages and creates incidentsSends notifications to Teams/SlackExposes metrics for PrometheusRuns in Docker with one commandThis tutorial will help you deploy a working monitoring system. However, there is more under the hood that we glossed over. In a second part we will talk about the following: prevent cascading failures when a service is flapping alert managers if the engineer on-call doesn't respond prevents notification storms check more frequently during incidents without restarting the service and compliance tracking
